title: A Qutrit Time Crystal Stabilized with Native Chiral Interactions
authors: Noah Goss, Nishchay Suri, Brian Marinelli, Larry Chen, Akel Hashim, Sajant Anand, Alexis Morvan, Ravi K. Naik, Ermal Rrapaj, David I. Santiago, Wibe de Jong, Norman Y. Yao, Joel E. Moore, Irfan Siddiqi
abstract: Periodically driven quantum many-body systems can spontaneously break discrete time-translation symmetry, realizing discrete time crystals. To date, both experimental and theoretical efforts have largely focused on the simplest case of spontaneous period-doubling in $\mathbb{Z}_2$ discrete time crystals realized with qubits. This owes, in part, to the challenge of stabilizing eigenstate order in higher discrete symmetry ($\mathbb{Z}_n$) time crystals, due to the presence of richer domain wall physics. Here, we demonstrate the realization of a $\mathbb{Z}_3$ discrete time crystal by implementing a Floquet chiral clock model in a chain of 15 superconducting qutrits. Unlike the conventional Ising setting, our system features a tunable chiral angle that governs domain-wall dynamics, spectral degeneracies, and crucially, the stability of time-crystalline order. Using disordered nearest-neighbor chiral interactions, we observe robust subharmonic period tripling that persists across a wide range of drive strengths and is independent of initial state. Finally, we highlight the special role that chirality plays in our $\mathbb{Z}_3$ discrete time crystal -- in its absence, the system's Floquet dynamics exhibit a marked initial state dependence governed by domain wall degeneracies. Our results establish native qudit hardware as a powerful platform to access a broader landscape of non-equilibrium phases.
summary: The researchers successfully realized a Z3 discrete time crystal using a chain of 15 superconducting qutrits. By utilizing native chiral interactions, they were able to stabilize period-tripling oscillations and prevent the destabilizing effects of domain wall degeneracies. This work highlights the potential of qudit-based processors for exploring complex non-equilibrium phases.
main_problem: The challenge of stabilizing higher discrete symmetry (Z3) discrete time crystals (DTCs) due to domain wall degeneracies that prevent the formation of stable, long-range order.
main_result: Demonstration of a robust Z3 DTC in a 15-qutrit superconducting chain, where native chiral interactions are shown to stabilize the period-tripling phase and prevent initial state dependence.
method: Implementation of a Floquet chiral clock model using a 20-transmon processor with tunable couplers, utilizing microwave-driven gates, flux-pulsing for interactions, and many-body localization (MBL) to prevent heating.
model_or_system: A 1D chain of 15 superconducting qutrits (spin-1) implemented on a 2D grid of 20 flux-tunable transmons and tunable couplers, following a Floquet chiral clock model with Z3 symmetry.
key_observables: Spin-1 magnetization, Fourier transform of magnetization (to detect period tripling), autocorrelators, and the Edwards-Anderson order parameter (chi_EA).
important_parameters: Kicking strength (g), chiral angle (theta), coupling strength (J), and system size (N).
main_assumptions: The system relies on many-body localization to prevent Floquet heating and assumes that the experimental decay is primarily limited by intrinsic system decoherence rather than gate errors.
figures_summary: Figures show the processor layout, coherence statistics, single-qutrit gate calibration workflow, magnetization spectroscopy (FFT), and the scaling of the Edwards-Anderson order parameter across different chain lengths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Z3 DTC stability, describes the superconducting qutrit hardware and chiral clock model implementation, details the calibration and control methods, presents the experimental observation of period tripling and the role of chirality, and concludes with phase transition analysis and scaling.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how engineering specific interaction symmetries (chirality) and utilizing higher-dimensional qudit (qutrit) hardware can access and stabilize exotic non-equilibrium phases of matter that are otherwise unstable in qubit-based systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental concepts. a, We engineer a spin-1 chain on a superconducting quantum processor composed of 20 flux-tunable transmons (orange) and tunable couplers (blue). Local “kicking” (red) is performed by charge driving (ˆn) coherent Rabi oscillations at the |0⟩↔|1⟩and |1⟩↔|2⟩transition frequencies. The disordered spin-spin interaction Hamiltonian Hint (light-blue) is realized from fast flux pulses on the tunable couplers (φext) which turns on a large dispersive coupling between the qutrits. b, A schematic illustration of the experimental circuits used to implement the Floquet Z3 CCM. Tuning from a Z2 to Z3 DTC. a, The spin-1 mag- netization vs. time FFT of one spin site in an 8 qutrit chain where our system is now driven with the modified global kick- ing ¯ Xg = ¯ X1−g 12 ¯ Xg. As can be observed from the FFT, for a small g, the system responds as a Z2 DTC with character- istic period doubling, before thermalizing at intermediate g, and finally recovering as a Z3 DTC at large g. b-c The qutrit state populations vs. Floquet cycle in respectively the Z2 and Z3 DTC regions.
